title: Heisenberg-limited Hamiltonian learning without short-time control
authors: Myeongjin Shin, Junseo Lee, Changhun Oh
abstract: Characterizing quantum systems by learning their underlying Hamiltonians is a central task in quantum information science. While recent algorithmic advances have achieved near-optimal efficiency in this task, they critically rely on accessing arbitrarily short-time dynamics. This reliance poses severe experimental challenges due to finite control bandwidth and transient pulse errors. In this work, we demonstrate that Heisenberg-limited Hamiltonian learning can be achieved without short-time control. We introduce a framework in which every query to the unknown dynamics has duration at least a prescribed minimum time $T$, and show that this restriction does not preclude Heisenberg-limited scaling. The key ingredient is a method for emulating the continuous quantum control required by iterative learning algorithms using only such lower-bounded evolution times. This reduces the learning task to sparse pure-state tomography. Notably, for logarithmically sparse Hamiltonians, our algorithm achieves the information-theoretically optimal $1/\varepsilon$ scaling in total evolution time for any arbitrary constant minimum evolution time $T$. For many-body (polynomially sparse) systems, we uncover a rigorous quantitative tradeoff, showing that the minimum required evolution time can be significantly relaxed from the standard limit at a polynomial cost in total evolution time. Our results affirmatively resolve a prominent open problem in the field and reveal that high-bandwidth, ultra-short pulses are not fundamentally necessary for optimal quantum learning.
summary: This paper demonstrates that optimal, Heisenberg-limited Hamiltonian learning is possible without needing ultra-short control pulses. By using a method to emulate short-time dynamics through longer evolution periods, the authors overcome experimental limitations related to finite control bandwidth.
main_problem: How to achieve Heisenberg-limited Hamiltonian learning when experimental constraints prevent the use of arbitrarily short-time evolution pulses.
main_result: The authors prove that Heisenberg-limited scaling (1/epsilon) can be achieved even with a minimum evolution time T > 0, resolving a major open problem. They establish a quantitative tradeoff between the minimum evolution time and total evolution time for many-body systems.
method: The framework emulates continuous quantum control by replacing short-time Trotter steps with long-time evolutions interleaved with learned correction unitaries, reducing the task to sparse pure-state tomography.
model_or_system: An n-qubit, m-sparse, traceless Hamiltonian composed of a sum of Pauli operators with unknown coefficients.
key_observables: Pauli coefficients of the Hamiltonian, total evolution time, and error in the operator norm.
important_parameters: Sparsity (logarithmic vs. polynomial), minimum evolution time (T), target accuracy (epsilon), and total evolution time (t_tot).
main_assumptions: The learner has oracle access to the time-evolution operator for durations at least T and can simulate the current Hamiltonian estimate.
figures_summary: Figure 1 contrasts previous short-time control approaches (relying on vanishingly small time steps) with the new framework using long-time evolutions and auxiliary operations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the short-time bottleneck problem, defines the long-time evolution framework, presents the emulation method for continuous control, analyzes the logarithmic sparsity regime, extends the results to polynomial sparsity via quasi-sparsity and BCH expansion, and concludes with complexity bounds.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for experimentalists in quantum computing and many-body dynamics because it proves that high-bandwidth, ultra-short pulses are not a fundamental requirement for optimal Hamiltonian characterization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Comparison between previous short-time control approaches and our framework. Previous Heisenberg- limited algorithms rely on stroboscopic control and Trotterization, requiring many ultra-short evolution segments with small 𝑡min. Our framework replaces these short-time queries by long-time evolutions interleaved with learned auxiliary operations, ensuring that every query to the unknown Hamiltonian has duration at least 𝑇. Thus, the short-time bottleneck is removed while Heisenberg-limited scaling in total evolution time is retained.

Figure 2. Schematic overview of our iterative learning protocol. At iteration 𝑗, the current estimate 𝐻𝑗 defines a residual Hamiltonian Δ𝐻𝑗= 𝐻−𝐻𝑗. We first learn an auxiliary Hamiltonian 𝑊𝑗using only long-time evolutions under 𝐻, and then use 𝑊𝑗to emulate the residual-control primitive 𝑒−𝑖Δ𝐻𝑗𝑡. This emulated evolution reduces coefficient extraction to sparse pure-state tomography. After estimating Δ𝐻𝑗, we update and truncate the estimate to obtain 𝐻𝑗+1, preserving 𝑚-sparsity and bounded norm while halving the error.
